core_claim: UnDefend (CVE-2026-45498) is a user-mode denial-of-service tool that holds Defender's signature-update files open so new definitions cannot load, leaving Defender stale while its status display still appears healthy. Released by Nightmare Eclipse in April 2026 as the third in a series of three Defender exploits; patched May 21, 2026.

caption:
Nightmare Eclipse released UnDefend as the third exploit in their April 2026 series. CVE-2026-45498 is a user-mode denial-of-service vulnerability that prevents Microsoft Defender from loading new signature definitions, leaving it stale while its own status display still reads healthy.

The mechanism is a file-locking race. UnDefend watches Defender's definition update directory using directory change notifications. When a new definition file appears, it races to open and lock the file before Defender can use it. Backup definition files are locked immediately at startup. No kernel driver, no network interception. Standard user access is enough.

Defender shows green. 'Protection up to date.' But the signature database is not updating. Definitions age silently. No alert fires immediately. The dashboard cannot verify what it is reporting. Defenders trust the green checkmark. The checkmark does not verify the engine.

The patch exists. Microsoft fixed CVE-2026-45498 in Defender Antimalware Platform version 4.18.26040.7, released May 21, 2026. CISA added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog the same day with a June 3 remediation deadline for federal agencies. Huntress confirmed active exploitation in a real intrusion in April 2026.

Defenders should confirm the platform version on all endpoints, alert when definition age exceeds 24 hours using a monitoring path independent of Defender's own status display, and treat stale definitions as an active incident signal.

A green checkmark is not proof of protection. It is a UI state that can be decoupled from the engine.

How do you verify Defender is actually updating and running?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[UnDefend, CVE-2026-45498, Nightmare Eclipse, Microsoft Defender, denial of service, definition updates, signature staleness, endpoint validation, CISA KEV, patch verification]
comment_prompt: How do you verify Defender is actually updating and running?
alt_text: Title card: UnDefend starves Microsoft Defender of its own signature updates. CVE-2026-45498 is a user-mode denial-of-service tool that holds Defender's definition files locked so new signatures cannot load, the third Nightmare Eclipse exploit released April 2026. A Defender shield's update pulse flatlines into a red line.

Nightmare Eclipse dropped UnDefend as the third exploit in April 2026, after BlueHammer and RedSun. CVE-2026-45498 holds Defender's definition files locked so signatures cannot load, on fully patched Windows, from a standard user account. A security panel reads healthy while the definition age grows.

UnDefend watches Defender's definition update directory using directory change notifications and races to lock each new file before Defender can read it. Backup definitions are locked at startup. Signatures never arrive. No kernel driver involved, no network interception. Two definition-file icons with red lock symbols.

The mechanism is a user-mode file-locking race. UnDefend opens and holds Defender's signature files before the update process can use them. The tool also watches for Defender service restarts and re-locks the signature base immediately when the service stops. A file being held open before a locked door.

Defender shows green and reports protection up to date, but definitions are aging silently. No alert fires immediately. The status display cannot verify what the engine is actually doing. Defenders trust the dashboard. A green dashboard with a faint red data stream leaking unseen.

Apply the patch: Defender Antimalware Platform version 4.18.26040.7 fixes CVE-2026-45498. Confirm the version on every endpoint. Alert when definition age exceeds 24 hours using monitoring independent of Defender's own status display. A split scene: a patched shield with a version label versus a stale one with an aging timestamp.
A green checkmark is not proof of protection. It is a UI state that can be decoupled from the engine. A Defender shield half glowing with a live pulse, half faded grey and unupdated.
The call to action asks how you verify Defender is actually updating and running. A monitoring view of Defender platform version and definition age on a clean dark end card.

sources.supports: CVE identifier, NVD published date 05/20/2026, CWE-400 (Uncontrolled Resource Consumption), Microsoft CVSS 4.0, affected platform Microsoft Defender Antimalware Platform, fixed in v4.18.26040.7, CISA KEV listing with June 3 2026 remediation deadline.

sources.source: Huntress: Nightmare-Eclipse Tooling Seen in Real-World Intrusion
sources.supports: User-mode file-locking mechanism (directory change notifications, file handle races on definition and backup files); no kernel driver involved; release as third exploit after BlueHammer and RedSun in April 2026; active exploitation observed April 2026; tamper-protection spoofing code was withheld by the researcher.

narration.text: In April 2026, a researcher named Nightmare Eclipse dropped a tool called UnDefend, and it prevents Microsoft Defender from loading updated signatures without triggering a single alert.
It was assigned CVE-2026-45498 and added to the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog in May 2026. Huntress confirmed real-world exploitation the same month.

The mechanism is a user-mode file-locking race. UnDefend watches Defender's definition directory and locks each new signature file before Defender can read it. No kernel driver, no network interception. Standard user access is enough.
It also locks backup definition files immediately at startup, and re-locks the signature base any time the Defender service stops and tries to restart. The signatures cannot load from any path.

Here is the dangerous part: Defender's status display still shows green, reports no threats, and claims protection is up to date, even while definitions are aging silently and no new threats can be detected.

The patch is out. Microsoft fixed this in Defender Antimalware Platform version 4.18.26040.7, released May 21, 2026. Confirm that version on every endpoint, and alert when definition age exceeds 24 hours using monitoring independent of Defender's own status UI.
A green checkmark is a UI state, not proof that the engine is actually updating. Those two things can be completely decoupled from each other.
How do you verify that Defender is actually updating and running on your endpoints? Drop it in the comments.

beats.caption: UnDefend starves Defender of its own updates. CVE-2026-45498.
Third exploit. April 2026. CVE-2026-45498. CISA KEV.
File-lock race. Definition directory watched. Signatures blocked.
User-mode. No kernel driver. Standard user. Backup files too.
Green checkmark. Definitions aging. Status display cannot verify.
Patch to 4.18.26040.7. Alert definition age. Independent monitoring.
Green checkmark is UI state. Not proof of protection.
